focus: routing-decision correctness and the savings-vs-quality tradeoff

node.strong_tier_backend: cli:claude (claude-opus-4-8, no API key / no per-token price)

methodology: Probes hit POST /v1/chat/completions with hibrid options; routing read from response.hibrid.chosen; tokens from response.usage. 180s timeout, retry-on-5xx, ~1s sleep between calls (imitating token_savings_run.py). The 2-core engine was under concurrent load from two other agents; 3 calls (batch_local, pii_phone_ssn_local, force_local) plus 1 SVQ-auto (svq_listcomp) returned transient 502 Bad Gateway after retries and were re-run successfully (agent3_retry.json). Strong tier has no dollar price, so savings are expressed as frontier-tier tokens avoided.

correctness_matrix.note: FAIL vs the stated preference. 'simple' is NOT a hard override: its policy ladder is local_free -> paid_cheap and the utility argmax picked paid_cheap. Consistently reproduced (the 3 'simple' SVQ tasks also routed paid_cheap). Honest finding: the router does NOT keep simple tasks local in this deployment.

correctness_matrix.intent: loop_refine never jumps to paid_strong even when complex
hard one-shot deep_reason is allowed to escalate to a paid tier
correctness_matrix.note: Escalated off local to a paid tier as intended; landed on paid_cheap rather than paid_strong (utility favored cheap for this prompt). Escalation rule satisfied.

correctness_matrix.intent: email PII forces local even with task_type=deep_reason
SSN-like number forces local even with task_type=deep_reason
credit-card-like number forces local even with task_type=deep_reason
allow_cloud=false on a hard task stays local (offline/private mode)
force cloud_strong honored on a trivial prompt
force local honored on a hard deep_reason prompt

correctness_summary.verdict: All HARD overrides are correct: PII (email/SSN/card) -> local regardless of task_type; allow_cloud=false -> local; force local/cheap/strong -> honored; loop_refine never escalates to the strong tier; deep_reason is allowed to escalate. The single failure is the SOFT preference that 'simple' tasks stay local: under the utility model the cheap paid tier wins, so simple tasks route to paid_cheap, not local_free.

savings_vs_quality.definition: frontier = paid_strong tier. 'WITH hibrid' = auto routing. 'WITHOUT' = every task forced to cloud_strong. Quality judged by objective checks (correct code / correct classification / correct extraction) on the actual answer text.

rows.note: Both produce `def add(a,b): return a+b`. Identical correctness.

rows.prompt: Refactor to a single list comprehension
rows.note: Local: [x**2 for x in xs if x>0]; strong: [x*x for x in xs if x>0]. Both correct. (Auto 502'd on first pass, correct on re-run.)
Both use s[::-1]. Strong adds explanatory prose (118 tok) the local answer omits (57 tok); the code is identical and correct.

rows.prompt: Translate to Spanish: 'The build is green.'
rows.note: Auto did NOT stay local (routed paid_cheap/haiku). Both gave a correct, idiomatic Spanish rendering.

rows.prompt: Classify sentiment of 'this library saved me hours'
rows.note: Both answered exactly 'positive'. Auto routed paid_cheap, not local.
Both faithful one-sentence summaries. Auto routed paid_cheap, not local.

savings_vs_quality.verdict: On every task it kept off the frontier, the router lost NO answer quality: all 6 auto answers passed the same objective checks as the forced-strong answers (correct code, correct 'positive', faithful summary/translation). Auto sent 0 of these 6 to the frontier (strong) tier, avoiding all 384 frontier tokens the all-strong counterfactual would have spent. The honest nuance: only the 3 loop_refine tasks stayed fully local; the 3 'simple' tasks went to the cheap paid tier (haiku), not local. So the saving here is mostly 'frontier avoided', and partly (3/6) 'fully local'. No case observed where the local/cheap answer was worse than strong.

engine_metrics.note: avg_latency rose to ~16s under the 3-agent concurrent load on 2 cores; this is the cause of the transient 502s on slow local calls, not a routing defect.

web_research.point: FrugalGPT frames cost saving via an LLM cascade: try cheap models first, escalate to expensive ones only when a learned scorer judges the cheap answer inadequate. Reports matching GPT-4 quality with up to ~98% cost reduction on their benchmarks - the canonical 'save money without losing quality' result. hibrid is a predictive (pre-execution) router rather than a post-hoc cascade, but shares the local/cheap-first, escalate-the-hard-ones principle.

Hybrid LLM (Ding et al., ICLR 2024) trains a router that sends queries to a small vs large model based on predicted difficulty, with a tunable quality target: up to ~40% fewer large-model calls at NO measured quality drop. Directly supports our finding that easy tasks can be kept off the frontier without quality loss.

RouterBench (Hu et al., 2024; UC Berkeley) is the standard 405k-outcome benchmark for evaluating routers on the cost-quality plane, using convex-hull / AIQ (area-under-quality-cost) metrics. The field's lesson: never report cost alone - report quality AT a cost, because no single model dominates across tasks.

Confidence-based model cascades use the small model's output uncertainty as the escalation signal (high-confidence = keep local; low-confidence = escalate to cloud). Surveys report cascades/routing cutting cost ~45-85% while retaining ~95% of quality. hibrid escalates on cheap pre-execution features (complexity heuristic, task_type, PII) rather than on output confidence - cheaper to compute but blind to whether the local answer was actually good.

Practitioner consensus (e.g. RouterBench/Martian write-ups): the strongest setups route first to avoid obviously mismatched tiers, then cascade within a tier; and hard privacy/compliance constraints (keep PII local) are treated as overrides outside the cost-quality optimization - exactly the layering hibrid implements with its PII->local and allow_cloud=false hard gates.
